Egypt's geographically compact, temple-anchored storage system allowed contraction without structural disintegration because surplus mobilization did not depend on long-distance exchange reciprocity.","scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "comparative Egyptian resilience"],"excludes": ["Sea Peoples as primary cause","climate determinism","Egypt proper internal transformation beyond contraction","Bronze Age Aegean pre-palatial societies"]},"forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence demonstrates functioning non-palace market exchange or craft production in Mycenaean territories after palace collapse (e.g., Tiryns LH IIIC subsistence autonomy)","eval": "rubric:std-hist"},{"case": "Hittite central institutions show successful decentralization into neo-Hittite successor polities without total collapse","eval": "rubric:std-hist"},{"case": "Egypt's survival attributed to identical palace-redistribution structure rather than distinct temple-storage geography","eval": "rubric:std-hist"}],"prose_notes": "Emphasizes institutional rigidity as endogenous property; treats Egyptian resilience as analogous structural conservatism rather than different causal pathway. Typicality: high among institutionalist archaeology (Bennet, Nakassis), but pushes monocausal exclusivity against multi-factor consensus."}

{"claim": "Palace-specific fragility from centralized storage and craft monopoly made economies hostage to elite bargaining breakdown", "mechanism": "Mycenaean and Hittite polities concentrated surplus in central magazines and controlled specialist production; when elites disputed succession or redistributive shares, the entire extractive apparatus became non-functional because no market or local substitution existed; Egypt's temple and nome-based parallel storage provided redundancy that palace-centric Anatolian/Greek systems lacked", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit"], "excludes": ["Levantine city-states (partially commercial)", "Egypt", "environmental triggers"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence of thriving local/non-palatial craft production and exchange continuing after palace destruction at multiple sites", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Hittite collapse shows military pressure preceding internal elite fragmentation in records", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Ugarit destruction layer contains no evidence of internal elite conflict preceding destruction; commercial archives suggest continued negotiation until abrupt end", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Emphasizes institutional architecture over external shock; notably omits Levantine cases and environmental factors per scope"}

{"claim": "The kīdugû and MAL.KARAŠTUM ritual terminologies circulating in Hittite and Ugaritic diplomatic correspondence constituted a trans-regional anxiety-amplification system that delegitimized palace elites before material collapse", "mechanism": "Shared Akkadographic divination protocols encoded crisis-interpretation frameworks; when Hittite Great King Muwatalli II's 'Plague Prayers' redefined calamity as divine-abandonment-of-dynasty, this rhetorical template propagated through scribal networks, rendering subsequent rulers permanently illegitimation-vulnerable. Egypt's separate pharaonic-theological apparatus (the king as immanent cosmic order) created rhetorical immunity to this specific delegitimation vector.", "scope": {"covers": ["Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Egypt proper", "non-textual archaeological evidence"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Greek Linear B archives contain no comparable crisis-divination rhetoric or foreign-policy anxiety transmission", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Ugaritic letters demonstrate confident continued dynastic legitimacy until destruction without scribal pessimism or anticipatory abandonment", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Hittite royal ideology shows successful rehabilitation of 'abandoned' kings (e.g., Ḫattušili III's usurpation narrative) rather than permanent delegitimation", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Requires treating textual genres as performative causes rather than reflective symptoms; risks circularity if collapse itself produces the archive disappearance that would falsify. Strong dependence on Assmann-style 'cultural memory' theory not universally accepted in ANE studies."}

{"claim": "Mycenaean wanax-qa-si-re-u hierarchies and Hittite vassalage networks developed structural commitment problems in alliance contracting: mutual-defense pacts created moral-hazard incentives for subsidiary elites to shirk obligations while extracting protection rents, and the sequential unraveling of Pylos-Thebes-Mycenae and Arzawa-Amurru-Hatti bonds propagated through reputation-destruction cascades, while Egypt's territorial compactness and pharaonic patrimonialism permitted direct monitoring without intermediary delegation.", "mechanism": "Network analysis of attested treaty and gift-exchange relations (Linear B 'e-qe-ta', Hittite 'ahhiyawa' and 'assuwa' documents, Amarna letters) reveals small-world topology with high betweenness-centrality nodes (Hatti, Mycenae); Bayesian updating on alliance reliability following isolated defections (Sea Peoples as endogenous recontracting option) triggered contagious renegotiation; Egyptian 'direct rule' model through nome-governors and military colonies minimized principal-agent slack.", "scope": {"covers": ["inter-palace diplomatic relations", "elite military obligations", "vassal renegotiation strategies"], "excludes": ["peasant agency", "climate forcing", "technological change", "Egyptian religious ideology as causal"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archival evidence shows no treaty ruptures or vassal defections preceding destruction horizons; destructions appear synchronized without documentary warning", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egypt maintained extensive vassal networks in the Levant (Ugarit, Amurru, Byblos) that collapsed simultaneously with Hittite and Mycenaean systems", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Network centrality measures do not correlate with destruction sequencing (peripheral Ugarit falls before central Hatti)", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian 'direct rule' relied heavily on Libyan and Nubian mercenary intermediaries with documented desertion and usurpation", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Middle-register institutionalist prose with formal-analytic borrowing (moral hazard, cascade, commitment problem); foregrounds strategic intentionality and bargaining failure; network-theoretic framing. Typical of political science/historical sociology syntheses (Morris, Manning, Brooke)."}

{"claim": "The 'Sea Peoples' were not an exogenous ethnic migration but the symptomatic expression of a novel military-technological complex (infantry with javelin, round shield, and defensive armor challenging chariot aristocracies) that diffused horizontally among displaced mercenaries, pirates, and rebellious coastal populations; this diffusion destabilized the peer-polity system by making palace military monopolies non-credible, triggering anticipatory defection by vassal rulers and mercenary bands who raided before central authority could reconstitute coercive advantage.", "mechanism": "Military innovation diffusion → commitment problem → anticipatory defection: The chariot-based palace armies faced an obsolescence spiral; once any polity's vassals or mercenaries acquired infantry tactics, all rulers faced the choice between preemptive concession (fragmentation) and risky rearmament (provoking the very revolt feared); the Hittite empire dissolved because its Syrian marcher lords could not credibly commit to non-defection, while Egypt survived because its Nubian gold income permitted sustained mercenary payment and its narrow frontages allowed defensive concentration.", "scope": {"covers": ["Sea Peoples as military-technological phenomenon", "Hittite vassal defection", "Syro-Hittite successor states", "Mycenaean martial culture transformation"], "excludes": ["Environmental factors as primary", "Internal class conflict in Egypt", "Long-term trade structural change"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "The Philistine and other 'Sea Peoples' material culture shows continuity with Mycenaean and Aegean traditions without evidence of novel military kit or tactics", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Hittite vassal treaties and letters show no anticipatory defection or renegotiation pressure in the decades before 1200 BC; Suppiluliuma II's campaigns appear offensive, not defensive", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian military papyri and reliefs depict unchanged chariot dominance with no infantry tactical innovation in response to Sea Peoples threats; Ramesses III wins by chariot counterattack", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "The Nubian gold mines were exhausted or abandoned by 1200 BC, with no documentary evidence of continued income underwriting mercenary retention", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Complement to A9 which emphasizes peasant agency; this centers elite military calculus. The 'obsolescence spiral' mechanism requires peer-polity isomorphism that may overstate Hittite-Mycenaean-Mitanni structural similarity."}

{"claim": "The collapse resulted from the rapid diffusion of inexpensive iron weaponry and new infantry tactics that democratized violence, rendering the chariot-based aristocratic monopoly on force obsolete.", "mechanism": "Technological disruption lowered the barrier to entry for warfare, allowing marginalized groups and mercenaries to overwhelm expensive, rigid professional armies reliant on bronze and chariots.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Cyprus", "Sea Peoples as a unified polity"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Evidence that iron weapons were widespread and decisive in battle prior to 1150 BC in the Levant", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "A classic but increasingly contested military-determinist view. It is marked atypical here because recent scholarship suggests iron was not yet dominant enough to cause such total systemic failure, and it struggles to explain why Egypt, which also faced these tactics, survived."}

{"claim": "The collapse was triggered by a cascading failure of international trade networks (the 'globalization' of the Bronze Age), where the loss of a single critical node (like Cyprus for copper) caused a systemic economic depression that destabilized all dependent palaces.", "mechanism": "Complex system fragility: interdependence meant that localized disruptions propagated rapidly across the entire network, causing simultaneous political fragmentation.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Cyprus"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Assyria", "inland agrarian communities"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Evidence that local production of bronze or essential goods continued uninterrupted in isolated palace centers after trade routes were cut", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "An atypical but increasingly popular systems-theory approach; it explains simultaneity well but requires assuming a higher degree of economic integration than some archaeological records support."}

{"claim": "The collapse was precipitated by the sudden proliferation of inexpensive iron weaponry among marginalized groups and mercenaries, which democratized violence and allowed numerically superior but previously inferior infantry to overwhelm the elite chariot-based aristocracies.", "mechanism": "Military technological disruption: The shift from expensive, state-controlled bronze armaments to locally smeltable iron broke the monopoly on violence held by palace elites, enabling decentralized rebel groups and sea-borne raiders to defeat professional armies.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Assyria", "Elam"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Metallurgical analysis proving iron artifacts in destruction layers were ceremonial or rare imports rather than mass-produced weapons", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This candidate challenges the traditional 'Sea Peoples' invasion narrative by focusing on internal class warfare enabled by technology, though it risks anachronism regarding the scale of iron production at this specific transition moment."}

{"claim":"Asymmetric diffusion of iron metallurgy created destabilizing military-technological gradient that dissolved palace monopolies on bronze-based coercion, enabling peripheral actors to dismantine centralized hierarchies","mechanism":"Iron ore ubiquity destroyed bronze's controlled-scarcity basis for elite military advantage; regions with prior metallurgical knowledge (Anatolia, Cyprus-adjacent Levant) experienced rapid weaponization diffusion that empowered disaffected mercenaries, raiders, and internal challengers; palace economies predicated on bronze redistribution became insolvent; Egypt retained coherence through Nilotic transport monopoly and existing mass-infantry tradition less dependent on elite metallurgical control","scope":{"covers":["Mycenaean Greece","Hittite Anatolia","Ugarit","Levantine city-states","Cyprus","Sea Peoples as iron-armed actors"],"excludes":["Egypt proper","Mesopotamian core","Interior Anatolian pastoralists without metallurgical access"]},"forbidden":[{"case":"Iron weapons dominate Sea Peoples weapon assemblages in destruction layers","eval":"rubric:std-hist"},{"case":"Hittite empire collapsed before significant iron weaponization in Anatolia","eval":"rubric:std-hist"},{"case":"Mycenaean palaces show systematic bronze armory abandonment in favor of iron decades pre-collapse","eval":"rubric:std-hist"},{"case":"Egyptian military texts emphasize bronze, not iron, superiority against Sea Peoples","eval":"rubric:std-hist"}],"prose_notes":"Materialist evolutionary-archaeological framework; treats technology as selective environment transforming institutional fitness landscapes; iron as disruptive innovation in Christensen sense applied to Bronze Age coercion"}

{"claim": "The collapse was driven by a rapid, disruptive shift in military technology and tactics—the massed infantry assault—which rendered the elite chariot-based armies of the palace states obsolete and allowed internal or external infantry forces to overwhelm the palatial centers.", "mechanism": "The development of new infantry weapons (like the Naue II slashing sword) and tactics (massed shock troops) negated the battlefield dominance of the chariot. Chariots were the military expression of the palatial elite's power and a key tool for suppressing internal revolt and defending borders. When infantry forces (whether Sea Peoples, rural rebels, or neighboring tribes) could defeat chariot armies, the palaces lost their coercive grip and were sacked in quick succession.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence shows that chariot use and production increased, not decreased, in the period immediately preceding the collapse.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "The destruction layers at collapsed sites show no evidence of infantry-type weapons or battle tactics distinct from earlier periods.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This account explains the military defeats and burn layers. Egypt survived because it adapted quickly, integrating infantry (including Sherden mercenaries) into its forces and using its infantry-based army to defeat the Sea Peoples at the Battle of the Delta, as recorded at Medinet Habu."}

{"claim": "A rapid, multi-year drought triggered a systemic famine that collapsed the specialized, interconnected palatial economies, which were brittle due to their reliance on surplus redistribution.", "mechanism": "The palatial system was a tightly coupled network of specialized production (oil, wine, textiles) and long-distance exchange. A severe climatic shock (drought) caused simultaneous harvest failures across the Eastern Mediterranean. The palaces, obligated to redistribute food, depleted their reserves and lost legitimacy, leading to internal revolt and the burning of administrative centers. Egypt survived because the Nile's predictable flood (fed by equatorial rains, not local precipitation) buffered it from the immediate agricultural shock, allowing the state to maintain grain reserves and social order.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "High-resolution paleoclimate proxies (e.g., from Soreq Cave, Israel) show no evidence of a significant, sustained drought event precisely coinciding with the 1200-1150 BC collapse horizon.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian records from the reign of Merneptah mention sending grain shipments to the Hittites, indicating that the Hittite famine was a localized political/economic failure, not a synchronous climatic event that Egypt was uniquely immune to.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This is a classic 'climate change' candidate, but it is made criticizable by specifying the exact mechanism (redistributive failure) and the precise reason for Egypt's survival (Nile's equatorial source). It is vulnerable to high-resolution paleoclimate data that might show asynchronous drought patterns or Egyptian records of aid that contradict the 'simultaneous collapse' narrative."}

{"claim": "Systems collapse was triggered by cascading failure of interdependent bronze-supply networks, where centralized palace economies optimized for military bronze consumption created brittle monocultures vulnerable to simultaneous Cypriot copper and distant tin supply disruptions","mechanism": "Network externality cascade: palace economies had evolved tight vertical integration (palace-controlled mining, smelting, and redistribution of bronze) that maximized short-term military competitiveness but eliminated redundant local metalworking and subsistence autonomy; when Cypriot copper production faltered and Anatolian/central Asian tin routes destabilized, the interdependence meant no single palace could adapt independently—attempted substitution to inferior arsenical copper failed militarily, triggering elite legitimacy crises, while Egypt's decentralized temple and local workshop economies maintained redundant copper-alloy traditions and Nubian gold circuits provided alternative prestige-resource flows","scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "collapse simultaneity", "Egyptian survival"], "excludes": ["Egypt proper beyond resilience comparison", "Minoan Crete as independent case", "Sea Peoples as primary movers"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeometallurgical surveys showing Cypriot copper production actually increased 1200-1150 BC while tin availability remained stable", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Evidence of arsenical bronze remaining militarily competitive against tin-bronze in Hittite or Mycenaean contexts post-1200", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Demonstration that Egyptian temple economies were equally centralized and interdependent with foreign bronze supply, lacking metallurgical redundancy", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Palace archives indicating diversified local metal procurement networks that should have buffered against Cypriot/tin disruption", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Builds on A2's mechanist demand; engages A6 by specifying Egypt's exclusion carefully—Nubian gold is mentioned only as alternative flow, not as structural explanation for Egypt's survival. Atypical in emphasizing technological substitution failure rather than climate or invasion."}

{"claim": "Egypt alone survived because its Late New Kingdom bureaucracy had achieved 'bureaucratic fission'—pre-existing parallel administrative tracks (vizierial, priestly, military, provincial) that could sustain territorial governance and resource extraction without pharaonic or palace-central coordination, whereas Eastern Mediterranean palace economies were 'bureaucratic singularity' systems whose administrative logic was inseparable from specific architectural nodes and dynastic persons.", "mechanism": "Comparative statecraft analysis: Egyptian institutional evolution through Middle Kingdom centralization, Second Intermediate Period fragmentation, and New Kingdom reconsolidation produced redundant governance capacities; Mycenaean and Hittite states remained 'personal-structural' fusions where administrative literacy, economic redistribution, and military mobilization required palace compound continuity; Ugarit and Levantine city-states lacked scale for redundancy. Crisis of 1200-1150 exposed this structural asymmetry: destruction of Hattusa or Pylos eliminated the state, whereas Ramesses XI's era of Libyan and priestly autonomy demonstrated that Egyptian governance could persist through 'decentralized recentralization' phases.", "scope": {"covers": ["Hittite state structure and its dissolution post-Hattusa", "Mycenaean wanax system and its disappearance", "Ugaritic royal merchant-palace synthesis", "Egyptian Twentieth-Twenty-First Dynasty transition"], "excludes": ["Climate determinants", "Sea Peoples as primary movers", "Technological or metallurgical factors", "Demographic collapse independent of institutional failure"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Egyptian evidence from Deir el-Medina, Theban tomb biographies, or Wilbour Papyrus demonstrates that New Kingdom governance collapsed uniformly and required full recentralization under Libyan or Kushite dynasties before administrative functions resumed.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Hittite or Mycenaean archives reveal non-palace administrative continuity (provincial governors, temple economies, independent merchant networks) maintaining regional governance for decades after palace destruction.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Comparative evidence shows Egyptian palace destructions (Amarna, Pi-Ramesses abandonment) produced equivalent state dissolution and multi-century recovery lags as Hittite or Mycenaean cases.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Archaeological surveys indicate substantial non-palatial settlement continuity in Greece and Anatolia, contradicting claim that palace destruction equaled total state elimination.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Explicitly reverses climate and migration priority: institutions as independent variable, material shocks as selectors among differentially resilient structures. 'Pharaonic exception' is not divine kingship but administrative archaeology—Egypt's depth of institutional layering. Comparative statecraft requires treating 'collapse' as variable outcome, not uniform phenomenon."}

{"claim": "A severe and geographically widespread megadrought driven by a rapid shift in the North Atlantic Oscillation caused systemic crop failure across palatial economies that lacked irrigation infrastructure, triggering state collapse through centralized redistribution failure.", "mechanism": "The Eastern Mediterranean palatial systems depended on rain-fed agriculture channeled through redistributive bureaucracies; a multi-decadal drought exceeding 30% precipitation reduction eliminated the surplus base, causing palace-dependent populations to fragment into mobile pastoralism. Egypt survived because the Nile flood regime, fed by equatorial African catchments independent of eastern Mediterranean precipitation patterns, maintained agricultural viability even with reduced flood levels.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt proper", "Mesopotamian riverine systems", "highland Anatolian pastoral economies", "climate variation outside 1250-1150 BC"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Palynological and speleothem records demonstrate no significant precipitation anomaly in Anatolia or Greece during 1250-1150 BC comparable to earlier Holocene drought events", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian records from the Twentieth Dynasty describe severe Nile failure and famine years causing institutional stress, including the strike at Deir el-Medina and the advent of the Sea Peoples", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Archaeological evidence shows continued palace functioning at Tiryns and Athens into the 11th century, with destruction layers selective rather than synchronous across the 1200-1150 window", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Emphasizes archaeological proxy data (δ¹⁸O, pollen, sediment cores) as primary evidence; treats climate as sufficient cause without addressing why similar Bronze Age climate fluctuations did not produce collapse."}

{"claim": "A rapid climatic aridification event caused simultaneous agricultural failure across the region.", "mechanism": "Environmental determinism where a 300-year drought exceeded the carrying capacity of palatial storage systems.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Cyprus"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Assyria", "Mesopotamia"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Paleoclimatic data indicating stable rainfall levels in Anatolia between 1200-1150 BC.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Contrasts with A7/A8 arguments; faces A9 critique regarding the geographical consistency of the migratory or environmental vector."}

{"claim": "Hittite and Mycenaean rulers pursued mutually destructive competitive-display diplomacy ('gift-economy escalation') that exhausted treasuries on monumental fortification and imported prestige goods, creating a Prisoners-Dilemma trap where unilateral restraint meant prestige-loss and internal coup; Egypt's more centralized theological legitimation permitted austerity without regime challenge.", "mechanism": "Institutional incentive distortion: interstate status competition → resource misallocation from productive infrastructure to conspicuous consumption → fiscal exhaustion → inability to respond to opportunistic Sea Peoples raids; comparative survival through Egypt's unique ability to convert economic retrenchment into religious virtue (Amun theology of pharaonic timelessness).", "scope": {"covers": ["Hittite-Anatolian competitive expenditure", "Mycenaean tholos-tomb and citadel escalation", "Ugarit as broker-dealer collapse", "Egyptian 20th Dynasty retrenchment"], "excludes": ["Climate factors", "Population pressure", "Technological change (iron)", "Egyptian Third Intermediate Period fragmentation"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeometric sourcing demonstrates Hittite and Mycenaean prestige-goods networks were self-sustaining through tribute extraction, not treasury-draining expenditures", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian 19th-20th Dynasty evidence shows substantial military expenditure on Libyan and Sea Peoples campaigns, not 'austerity' or theological retrenchment", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Ugarit archives indicate commercial profit-seeking rather than status-competition motives in elite exchange", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Draws on Moran 1987 gift-exchange analyses and Sherratt-Sherratt 1998 on consumption; deliberately atypical as voluntarist-institutional account in mechanist neighbourhood. Spec 2 demands actor-centric political register, which this supplies through strategic-choice framing contra A3 skeptic school's probable social-structural continuation."}

{"claim": "The collapse was caused by a rapid shift to massed mobile infantry (the 'Naue II' sword revolution) that rendered chariot-based palatial armies obsolete, enabling internal rebellions and external raids.", "mechanism": "Iron-working and new slashing swords allowed lightly armored infantry to overwhelm chariot corps, breaking the military monopoly of the palatial elite. Displaced mercenaries and 'Sea Peoples' exploited this tactical shift.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Egyptian records from the reign of Ramesses III describe the Sea Peoples as primarily chariot-based forces, not massed infantry.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Archaeological evidence shows no significant increase in Naue II sword finds in the Aegean and Anatolia until after the palatial destructions.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This account explains the simultaneity of collapse through a rapid military revolution that spread via mercenary networks. Egypt survived because its centralized state could adapt infantry tactics and leverage its geographic buffer, but the account struggles with the lack of evidence for infantry-dominant Sea People armies in Egyptian records."}

{"claim": "Climate-driven systems collapse via drought-induced state capacity erosion", "mechanism": "Severe multi-decadal drought (ca. 1200-1150 BC) reduced agricultural surplus below threshold for palace redistribution economies; Mycenaean, Hittite, and Levantine systems lacked Nile flood irrigation resilience, triggering cascading abandonment of administrative infrastructure, scribal discontinuity, and political fragmentation.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt proper", "Mesopotamia", "Cyprus", "Minoan Crete (pre-1450)"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Paleoclimate proxies show no anomalous aridity in Aegean/Anatolia 1250-1150 BC comparable to earlier Holocene fluctuations", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian collapse or fragmentation contemporaneous with Eastern Mediterranean palace destructions", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Continuity of Hittite state institutions in Anatolia post-1150 BC", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Prioritizes environmental determinism; assumes palace economies were marginally subsistent rather than buffered; treats Egyptian survival as hydraulic exceptionalism."}

{"claim": "A hyper-localized pandemic of ergotism or anthrax decimated the administrative core of palace societies.", "mechanism": "Contaminated grain stores or livestock led to mass mortality among the literate bureaucratic class, causing immediate administrative paralysis without widespread physical destruction.", "scope": {"covers": ["Hittite Anatolia", "Mycenaean Greece", "Ugarit"], "excludes": ["Rural villages", "Nomadic groups"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Skeletal remains from Pylos show no signs of epidemic disease or mass graves dated to 1200 BC", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Highly atypical speculative candidate; explains sudden loss of literacy and administration better than invasion theories."}

{"claim": "Elite ideological delegitimation and internal palace fragmentation", "mechanism": "Widespread adoption of alphabetic writing and ironworking technologies (previously palace monopolies) enabled peripheral nobles, merchants, and military commanders to bypass scribal bureaucracies and establish independent resource bases; coupled with dynastic instability and contested royal succession across multiple polities, this caused palace elites to lose coercive capacity over their territories, fragmenting into warlord competition; Egypt survived because its theocratic kingship ideology (divine embodiment in Pharaoh) retained sufficient charismatic legitimacy to suppress usurpation even under Libyan and Nubian dynastic challenges, and its desert borders made peripheral independence logistically harder than in mountainous Anatolia or Greece.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece (wanax collapse)", "Hittite Anatolia (vassal revolt)", "Ugarit (merchant oligarchy challenge)", "Levant (local dynasts)"], "excludes": ["Egypt (ideological continuity)", "non-palace social formations", "environmental or external-invasion monocausal accounts"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence shows no expansion of iron use or alphabetic literacy in palace peripheries before destruction layers", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian 20th Dynasty shows successful usurpations or regional fragmentation comparable to Hittite or Mycenaean cases", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Ugaritic archives indicate stable dynastic continuity until violent external destruction, with no internal elite challenge", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Internal-institutional mechanism emphasizing technological democratization and ideology; explicitly rejects monocausal invasion or climate accounts."}

{"claim": "The palace system suffered from increasing institutional brittleness due to coercive surplus extraction intensification (corvée, military obligation, craft specialization debt-peonage) that eroded peasant compliance margins; simultaneous elite fragmentation into competing patronage networks disabled coordinated response to perturbations of ordinary magnitude, producing cascading abandonment as provincial sites preemptively defected.", "mechanism": "Institutional fragility through surplus-extraction ratcheting: comparative analysis of Linear B, Hittite land-grant, and Ugaritic texts shows rising administrative overhead and military commitment without corresponding legitimacy mechanisms; this created a tipping-point structure where small exogenous shocks (raiding, minor drought, palace succession crisis) triggered large endogenous cascades because local actors' best response to uncertainty was non-compliance; Egypt survived because its bureaucratic-religious integration (temple-corvee, divine kingship) maintained coordination despite similar extraction pressures.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "institutional structures", "1200-1150 BC timing"], "excludes": ["Environmental/climate determinants as primary drivers", "Sea Peoples as unified migration wave", "technological failure (bronze shortage, iron transition)", "Egypt's survival as requiring no explanation (to be explained)"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Linear B and Hittite archives show declining administrative complexity and decentralization from c. 1350 BC, indicating adaptive flexibility rather than brittle intensification", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian New Kingdom institutions exhibit identical or greater extraction rates with comparable elite fragmentation (Amarna period, post-Ramesses III Harim conspiracy) without systemic collapse", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Archaeological settlement patterns show no increase in rural/site abandonment preceding palace destruction horizons", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Hittite vassal treaties and Mycenaean wanax ideology demonstrate robust legitimacy mechanisms functioning until terminal destruction layers", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Comparative-history institutionalist model; draws on Joseph Tainter and Michael Hudson but modifies with peer-polity competitive pressure; treats Egypt as requiring explanation not exception."}

{"claim": "Systemic fragility due to hyper-specialization and just-in-time resource distribution collapsed under minor shocks.", "mechanism": "Complexity theory: tightly coupled networks lack redundancy, causing cascading failure from localized disruptions.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Mesopotamia", "Assyria"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence of significant grain stockpiles in Pylos prior to destruction", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Relies on A1 empiric stance; contrasts with A5 by focusing on network topology rather than bureaucratic rigidity alone."}

{"claim": "Collapse was caused by a synchronous failure of bronze-alloy supply chains due to exhaustion of Cypriot copper-ore proximate sources, depriving palace economies of military and agricultural tools essential to surplus extraction and state maintenance","mechanism": "The palace-states operated as redistributive bronze-command economies where tin-bronze metallurgy underpinned elite military monopoly and agricultural intensification; Cyprus was the proximate copper source for all four regions; ore-quality decline and transport-cost spirals after 1250 BC caused simultaneous de-alloying crises that made palaces unable to arm retinues or provide farmers with tools, triggering cascading abandonment of state-dependent settlement patterns","scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece","Hittite Anatolia","Ugarit","Levantine city-states"],"excludes": ["Egypt proper (Nile Valley)","Mesopotamian heartland","Iranian plateau","Cyprus as independent polity"]},"forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence shows Cypriot copper production and export actually intensified post-1200 BC with 'Post-Palatial' or 'Protohistoric' copper-working continuing without interruption","eval": "rubric:std-hist"},{"case":"Bronze tools found in destruction layers and post-collapse strata show no compositional shift toward arsenical copper or recycling crises; tin continued to reach Aegean and Levantine workshops","eval":"rubric:std-hist"},{"case":"Collapse was staggered over ~50 years (Hittites ~1180, Ugarit ~1200, Mycenaean centers ~1150-1050), not synchronous as bronze-chain failure would predict","eval":"rubric:std-hist"}],"prose_notes": "Emphasizes material-determinist mechanism avoiding climate and migration explanations; deliberately narrow to bronze metallurgy to invite critique about monocausality; forbidden cases target chronological and compositional evidence"}

{"claim": "Systemic fragility of hyper-specialized palatial economies triggered a cascading failure upon the disruption of any single node.", "mechanism": "The Late Bronze Age civilizations operated as a tightly coupled, just-in-time supply chain for bronze (copper from Cyprus, tin from Afghanistan). When maritime trade routes were severed by initial shocks (earthquakes or raids), the inability to import tin halted bronze production, crippling military and agricultural capacity, leading to rapid internal collapse and abandonment of centers.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Cyprus"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Assyria", "Mesopotamia proper"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence of significant local tin stockpiles or alternative bronze production methods sustaining palaces for decades after trade cessation.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This is the modal 'systems collapse' theory, highly typical in current historiography, emphasizing economic interdependence over single causes."}

{"claim": "The Late Bronze Age collapse was caused by a systemic failure of the interdependent palatial trade and diplomacy network, triggered by a cascade of disruptions that the redistributive palace economies were too rigid to absorb.", "mechanism": "Palaces operated as centralized hubs for collection, storage, and redistribution of agricultural surplus and luxury goods, and for organizing long-distance trade. This created a tightly coupled system where a shock to one node (e.g., crop failure, earthquake, or trade route disruption) would cascade through the network. When multiple shocks hit simultaneously, the administrative and economic complexity of the palaces became a liability: they could not adapt quickly enough, leading to a loss of faith in the elite, abandonment of the palaces, and a systemic collapse of the entire Eastern Mediterranean network.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Evidence of a single, simultaneous military conquest across all regions by a unified external enemy (e.g., a single 'Sea Peoples' army).", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Evidence that all affected regions experienced identical, simultaneous, and total environmental catastrophes (e.g., a single mega-drought) with no regional variation.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Egypt survived because its geography (the Nile) provided a more resilient agricultural base and a more unified state structure that could absorb shocks and repel the disparate 'Sea Peoples' migrations, which were a symptom, not the root cause, of the systemic collapse."}

{"claim": "The collapse was caused by a rapid shift to massed infantry warfare that rendered chariot-based palatial armies obsolete.", "mechanism": "Iron metallurgy and new infantry tactics enabled mass mobilization of commoners, overwhelming elite chariot corps and destabilizing palace-centered military aristocracies.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Egyptian records show no adoption of infantry-based tactics or iron weapons during this period, and their chariot forces remained dominant against the Sea Peoples.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Explains why palace systems dependent on chariot elites fell while Egypt, which repelled Sea Peoples using combined arms including chariots, survived. The military revolution hypothesis accounts for the rapidity and selectivity of collapse."}

{"claim": "The Eastern Mediterranean palace system constituted a scale-free trade network whose collapse followed from targeted destruction of highest-betweenness centrality nodes (Ugarit, Emar) by agents who did not comprehend network structure, triggering topological cascades that propagated faster than institutional adaptation permitted.", "mechanism": "Maritime-anarchic raiding (Sea Peoples and equivalent) disproportionately destroyed entrepôt cities whose topological role exceeded their geopolitical significance; network homophily in bullion-weight standards and gift-exchange protocols prevented modular substitutability, so path-length explosions in surviving subnetworks paralyzed information and commodity transmission, causing coordinated abandonment before physical destruction reached peripheral nodes.", "scope": {"covers": ["Ugarit", "Levantine emporia", "Mycenaean redistribution", "Hittite interdependence"], "excludes": ["Egyptian survival as network outcome", "climate as primary driver", "single polity endogenous decline"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence shows Mycenaean palace destructions preceded Ugarit's destruction by decades, contradicting synchronized cascade prediction.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "The Hittite empire was minimally integrated into Aegean-Levantine maritime networks; its collapse requires distinct mechanism not network-transmitted.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian survival reflects identical network position (Thebes-Memphis-Ramesse-Sile as high-centrality nodes) that should have produced equivalent vulnerability per mechanism.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Post-collapse Cyprus and Cilicia maintain metallurgical trade demonstrating network substitutability and modular recovery.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Formally abstract in importing network science topology without Bronze Age-specific edge-weight or node-capacity parameterization. The mechanism requires treating Hittite Anatolia as network-integrated despite minimal direct maritime connectivity; this is defended via indirect entanglement (silver, tin, diplomatic marriage) but stretches the formalism. Atypical in denying Egypt special treatment—mechanism must assert Egypt was attacked but topologically resilient, which is ad hoc."}

{"claim": "A ~300-year abrupt megadrought (11.7-8.9 kyr BP analog, confirmed via Levantine Dead Sea levels and Aegean speleothem δ18O) caused synchronized crop failures across palatial breadbaskets, collapsing tax bases and triggering palace-burning revolts","mechanism": "Climate-forced state-fiscal crisis: megadrought → grain yield collapse → surplus extraction failure → military-pay and ration-system breakdown → elite-retainer defection → urban conflagration by starving populations; Egypt survived due to Nile flood regime independence from Levantine rainfall","scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt proper", "Mesopotamian polities", "Minoan Crete pre-1450"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Egyptian New Kingdom state archives record contemporaneous Nile failure and grain import dependency from Levant, contradicting hydraulic autonomy", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Paleoclimate proxies show megadrought onset post-dating earliest palace destructions by 50+ years, reversing causal order", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Archaeobotanical assemblages show no crop substitution or adaptive shifts expected under prolonged drought stress", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Linear B archives document military disbursements continuing until final destruction, contradicting pay-system collapse", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Annalistic-empirical framing; treats 1200 BC as hard synchronism; relies on Roberts et al. 2011, Kaniewski et al. 2013 drought correlations; vulnerability in dating precision and Egyptian grain-trade records"}

{"claim": "The palace systems collapsed because they were overcentralized brittle hierarchies with no mechanisms for devolving authority; Egypt survived not through superior resources but through its pre-existing tradition of provincial nomarch autonomy and the New Kingdom's experimental grant of military fiefs (iry-pdt), which created resilient sub-elites capable of maintaining local order when central capacity contracted.", "mechanism": "Institutional design difference: Mycenaean wanax-to-damokoi chains, Hittite royal land management through palace servants (DUMU.E.GAL), and Ugaritic royal monopoly on overseas trade created single points of failure. Collapse propagated when central loci failed because no legitimate intermediate authority existed. Egypt's structural heterogeneity—temple estates, nomarchic traditions, and Ramesside military settlements—permitted 'graceful degradation': the Third Intermediate Period was fragmentation, not disappearance, because local power holders possessed recognized legitimacy and extraction capacity. Nubian gold and Levantine tribute were symptoms of this resilience, not causes.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean palatial hierarchy and its absence below wanax"], "excludes": ["Egyptian 'prosperity' or cultural continuity as unified state", "Climate or environmental determinants as primary", "Sea Peoples as independent sufficient cause", "Technological or economic factors outside institutional design"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence demonstrates robust sub-palatial elite continuity in Mycenaean Greece post-1200, with local big-man or chieftain institutions maintaining social order and material culture.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian post-1200 fragmentation (Third Intermediate Period) shows comparable loss of literacy, monumental construction, and interregional coordination to Greek or Anatolian 'Dark Ages,' contradicting claims of differential resilience.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Hittite vassal treaties or Mycenaean damokoi archives indicate delegated authority with local resource autonomy comparable to Egyptian nomarchs.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Policy-memorandum framing; treats collapse as organizational failure and survival as institutional optionality. Counterintuitively deemphasizes Egypt's geographic or material advantages. Must confront apparent equivalence of all regions' post-collapse 'darkness'—distinction may be overstated."}

{"claim": "A severe and prolonged mega-drought, evidenced in paleoclimate records, caused cascading agricultural failures across the Eastern Mediterranean, leading to famine, social unrest, and the collapse of interconnected palatial economies.", "mechanism": "Climatic forcing: A multi-decadal aridification trend reduced crop yields below subsistence thresholds, triggering famine, mass migration, and the abandonment of administrative centers. The highly specialized, redistributive palatial economies lacked the resilience to adapt to rapid environmental change.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Paleoclimate proxies (e.g., pollen, speleothems, sediment cores) from the affected regions show no significant deviation in precipitation or temperature during the 13th-12th centuries BC.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This is a classic environmental determinism account. It explains the simultaneous, widespread nature of the collapse through a shared climatic event. Egypt's survival is attributed to the Nile's predictable, non-rainfall-dependent flood cycle, which buffered it against the drought that devastated rain-fed agricultural systems elsewhere."}

{"claim": "A pandemic swept through densely populated palatial centers, causing administrative collapse and social disintegration.", "mechanism": "A virulent pathogen spread along trade routes, hitting urbanized palace complexes hardest and leaving survivors unable to maintain complex economic and political structures.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Egypt's dense Nile delta population and trade connections would have made it equally susceptible to a pandemic spreading along the same routes.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Egypt's survival is attributed to its more dispersed rural population along the Nile and superior medical knowledge, though this is a weak exclusion."}

{"claim": "The collapse resulted from a disruptive shift in military technology and tactics, specifically the rise of inexpensive infantry equipped with javelins and long swords defeating expensive chariot-based aristocracies.", "mechanism": "Decentralized groups utilizing new infantry tactics overwhelmed the rigid, chariot-dependent military structures of the palace states, leading to rapid political disintegration.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Sea Peoples"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Assyria"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Evidence that chariot warfare remained the dominant and successful military paradigm in the region after 1175 BC", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "A classic military-revolution hypothesis (e.g., Drews). It is slightly less typical now due to nuanced views on Sea Peoples, but remains a strong contender for the mechanism of destruction. Excludes Egypt as it successfully adapted its military mix."}

{"claim": "Palace economies exhibited structural hyper-fragility from successful administrative scaling: centralization of craft production, land tenure registration, and long-distance exchange created network topologies where targeted removal of scribal/palatial nodes caused irreversible information loss about obligations, entitlements, and technical procedures, with collapse persisting because no non-palatial institution could replicate this coordinating knowledge","mechanism": "Network cascade failure: cuneiform and Linear B administration encoded relational databases (personnel, land, tribute, craft schedules) in non-portable institutional memory; palace destruction eliminated master-scribal training apparatus; surviving populations retained material techniques (potting, metallurgy) but lost capacity to coordinate multi-regional division of labor; 'collapse' was therefore coordination failure not production failure—post-palatial Greece and Anatolia show continued material culture but at smaller scale with loss of literacy, standardization, and architectural monumentality; Egypt survived because its administrative class was temple-embedded with dispersed mortuary cult endowments that survived political transitions (First Intermediate, Second Intermediate precedents), and because hieroglyphic training occurred in multiple temple centers rather than single palatial nodes","scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt proper (temple-embedded scribalism with distributed replication)", "Mesopotamian polities with temple-palace dual administration"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Post-palatial Crete (LM IIIC) and Tiryns show immediate reconstitution of regional administrative infrastructure using modified Linear B or derivative scripts", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "The Hittite empire demonstrates continuity of legal traditions, land tenure records, and cultic calendars in Syro-Hittite successor states with direct textual lineage from Hattusa archives", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Cypro-Minoan script shows adaptation for administration in Cypriot polities post-1190 BC, indicating successful non-palatial scribal transmission in Eastern Mediterranean context", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egypt's 20th Dynasty shows identical centralization vulnerability: Thebes and Pi-Ramesses scribal apparatus was concentrated with collapse of royal patronage under Ramesses XI", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Emphasizes information-theoretic fragility over material constraints; treats Egypt contrast as institutional-design difference not scale or wealth difference; draws on Yoffee 2005, Ur 2014 but specifies mechanism more precisely"}

{"claim": "The Late Bronze Age collapse was caused by a cascading failure of the inter-palatial diplomatic and trade network, where the destruction of one major node triggered a catastrophic breakdown of the entire system of reciprocal exchange and communication.", "mechanism": "Palaces were economically interdependent for prestige goods, raw materials, and grain. When one or two key hubs (e.g., Hattusa, Mycenae) were destroyed by internal revolt or external raid, the loss of trade partners and diplomatic guarantees caused a domino effect of economic strangulation and political delegitimization across the network.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence shows that trade between surviving and collapsed regions continued uninterrupted after the initial destructions.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Textual evidence reveals that the palatial administrations had already diversified their economies away from long-distance trade before the collapse.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This account treats the Eastern Mediterranean as a tightly coupled system. Egypt's survival is attributed to its greater agricultural self-sufficiency and the Nile's role as a unified internal trade route, making it less dependent on the fragile maritime network. The account is refuted if evidence shows the network was not tightly coupled or that collapse was not simultaneous."}

{"claim": "The collapse was caused by a rapid shift in military technology, specifically the adoption of massed infantry with javelins and long swords, which rendered chariot-based palatial armies obsolete.", "mechanism": "Peripheral mercenaries and pastoralist groups, familiar with infantry tactics from the fringes of the palatial world, exploited the tactical weakness of chariots on broken terrain. They overwhelmed the palaces, severed trade routes, and triggered a domino effect of economic collapse and internal rebellion.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence shows no significant change in weaponry or armor in the Aegean and Near East during the 13th-12th centuries BC, with chariots remaining the dominant military arm until after the collapse.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian records from the period detail large-scale chariot battles against the Sea Peoples, indicating the threat was not based on infantry superiority.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This account focuses on a single, dramatic military revolution. It explains Egypt's survival by its rapid adoption of the same mercenary infantry tactics, integrating Sherden and other groups into its army, thus neutralizing the threat."}

{"claim": "The collapse resulted from the sudden proliferation of inexpensive iron weaponry democratizing violence against bronze-dependent elites.", "mechanism": "New metallurgical techniques allowed non-state actors to equip large infantry forces capable of defeating chariot aristocracies.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Sea Peoples"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Cyprus", "Assyria"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Dated artifacts show iron weapons remained rare ritual objects until after 1100 BC.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Popular in older historiography; increasingly criticized by A6 for anachronistically projecting later Iron Age military realities onto the Late Bronze Age transition."}

{"claim": "The collapse was triggered by a sudden, cascading failure of the bronze trade network due to a supply shock, which dismantled the ideological and military basis of palatial authority.", "mechanism": "Palatial elites depended on bronze for both weapons (military power) and prestige goods (ideological power). A disruption in the supply of tin (possibly from Central Asia or the West) made bronze production impossible. This simultaneously crippled the military capacity of the palaces and destroyed their ability to perform the gift-exchange rituals that maintained the network's diplomatic cohesion. Egypt survived because it controlled local gold mines and could substitute gold for bronze in its prestige economy, while its grain-based economy could sustain a defensive military posture long enough to adapt.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence shows that bronze production and recycling continued uninterrupted in the Eastern Mediterranean throughout the 12th century BC, with no significant drop in tin content in surviving artifacts.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian records from the reign of Ramesses III describe receiving tribute and trade goods, including metals, from surviving networks, indicating the trade routes were not entirely severed.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This candidate proposes a systemic, materialist cause rooted in the specific resource dependencies of the Bronze Age. It is highly criticizable because it requires proving a specific, synchronous tin supply shock, which is difficult with current archaeological data. It also must explain why local recycling couldn't buffer the shock."}

{"claim": "The collapse was a contingent outcome of a specific migratory domino effect initiated by displacement in the Central Balkans, which propagated instability eastward and southward only where political integration was shallow.", "mechanism": "Kinetic chain reaction: displaced populations (Sea Peoples) acted as a shockwave; societies with deep vertical integration (Egypt) absorbed the shock via mobilization, while horizontally linked but vertically weak palace networks (Ugarit, Mycenae) fractured under the pressure of refugee influx and mercenary betrayal.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Hittite Anatolia"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Assyria", "Babylonia"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Historical record of coordinated, simultaneous multi-front attacks by Sea Peoples rather than sequential migration waves", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "An atypical candidate focusing on the vector of migration and the specific structural vulnerability of 'shallow' integration compared to Egypt's 'deep' state capacity. It challenges the idea of a uniform cause, emphasizing the transmission mechanism over the root trigger."}

{"claim": "The collapse was caused by a systemic failure of the palatial redistribution economy due to prolonged drought and famine, which severed the resource flows necessary to maintain centralized authority.", "mechanism": "Climate-induced agricultural collapse led to food shortages, triggering internal revolts and the abandonment of palace centers that could no longer feed their populations or pay retainers.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Mesopotamia", "Assyria"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence of abundant grain stores in destroyed palace layers dated to 1200 BC", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This is a high-typicality candidate aligning with the dominant climate-change hypothesis (e.g., Kaniewski et al.), explaining the simultaneity via a regional weather event while excluding Egypt due to its reliance on the predictable Nile flood."}

{"claim": "The Late Bronze Age collapse was triggered by a rapid climatic shift to arid conditions that caused widespread harvest failures, which in turn destabilized the palatial redistributive economies.", "mechanism": "A mega-drought (supported by paleoclimatic proxies) reduced agricultural yields across the Eastern Mediterranean. The highly centralized palatial economies, dependent on surplus extraction and redistribution, could no longer sustain their administrative and military apparatus, leading to internal revolts and elite abandonment of administrative centers.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Paleoclimatic data shows a long-term wet period or stable precipitation across the Eastern Mediterranean from 1250-1100 BC.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "The palatial centers show clear evidence of gradual, peaceful abandonment over several generations rather than rapid, violent destruction.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This is a classic environmental determinist account. It explains the broad synchronicity of the collapse but struggles with Egypt's survival, which is often attributed to the Nile's unique flood-based agriculture being less susceptible to rainfall variation. The mechanism is systemic but relies heavily on a single external trigger."}

{"claim": "Intentional palace decentralization via entrepreneurial merchant defection", "mechanism": "Development of private maritime commerce (alphabetic writing, standardized weights) reduced information asymmetries enabling merchant elites to bypass palatial taxation; 'collapse' represents deliberate withdrawal of cooperation from redistribution apparatus, with simultaneous destructions reflecting coordinated tax resistance rather than systemic failure.", "scope": {"covers": ["Ugarit", "Levantine coast", "Cyprus", "Aegean"], "excludes": ["Hittite Anatolia", "Inland Mycenaean centers", "Egyptian state sector", "Nubian trade route"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Continuity of private commercial activity post-1150 BC without political fragmentation or settlement abandonment", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Evidence of palace intensification of control measures (debt records, corvée expansion) in decades pre-1200 BC", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Archaeological evidence of violent destruction by fire at Ugarit and other 'defecting' centers", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Anachronistically projects modern market exit onto Bronze Age political economy; treats destruction layers as epiphenomenal or misdated; assumes merchant class autonomy unsupported by textual evidence."}

{"claim": "The Sea Peoples were not the primary cause but rather the terminal symptom of a cascading failure initiated by external climate shocks that destroyed the cereal-trade network binding the palace economies", "mechanism": "The 12th-century BCE 'Rapid Climate Change' (RCC) episode, evidenced in speleothem and lake-bed records from the Soreq Cave and Lake Van, produced consecutive drought years that collapsed Anatolian and Aegean cereal yields. Palace economies had evolved toward interdependence: Mycenaean centers imported Anatolian grain, Hittite capitals imported Cypriot copper revenue for grain purchases, Ugarit intermediated Levantine-European shipping. Climate failure created a supply shock that propagated through debt-bondage, mercenary non-payment, and piracy emergence. The 'Sea Peoples' attested in Ramesses III's inscriptions were heterogeneous displaced populations—including former palace dependents and mercenaries—whose maritime raids were adaptive responses to system collapse rather than independent barbarian invasions. Egypt survived because the Nile's flood regime, fed by equatorial African rainfall unrelated to Eastern Mediterranean climate patterns, maintained agricultural autarchy, while its geographic chokepoints (Sinai, Nile mouths) permitted defensive concentration.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean palace economy collapse", "Hittite central authority dissolution", "Ugarit's destruction and abandonment", "Levantine city-state failures (Megiddo VIIB, Lachish VII)", "Sea Peoples phenomenon as dependent variable"], "excludes": ["Egypt proper (Nile Valley agriculture)", "Mesopotamian polities (Kassite Babylonia, Assyria)", "Minoan Crete (already collapsed c. 1450)"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "If Egyptian documentary or archaeological evidence showed simultaneous Nile failures or grain import dependency during 1200-1150 BCE", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "If palaeoclimate proxies demonstrated synchronous wet conditions or stable yields in Anatolia/Aegean during this period", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "If Ugaritic or Hittite archives revealed thriving inter-palace commerce and gift-exchange continuing until destruction without supply stress", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "If Sea Peoples identities could be traced to non-Mediterranean homelands with independent political organization predating the collapse period", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This candidate deliberately inverts the traditional 'invasion' narrative. It treats the Sea Peoples as endogenous to the system rather than exogenous shock, making it vulnerable to any evidence of their prior coherence. Typicality is suppressed because the mechanism foregrounds climate determinism, which the neighbourhood's skeptic stance (A1) and counterexample-first directive (weight 0.95) treat with suspicion."}

{"claim": "The collapse resulted from the obsolescence of chariot-based aristocratic warfare against decentralized, flexible infantry tactics employed by sea-borne raiders and internal dissidents.", "mechanism": "Tactical asymmetry: The high-cost, rigid chariot corps of the palace states could not respond to fast-moving, small-unit infantry attacks, leading to rapid military decapitation and loss of central authority.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Sea Peoples"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Assyria"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Evidence that Sea Peoples primarily utilized chariot warfare or that palace armies successfully deployed infantry as their primary defense prior to collapse", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Focuses on military sociology; explains the speed of collapse but requires assuming a sudden tactical shift by attackers that archaeological records only partially support."}

{"claim": "The simultaneity is an illusion caused by the fragility of complex supply chains for essential prestige goods (tin and copper), where a localized disruption in Cyprus triggered a continent-wide loss of elite legitimacy.", "mechanism": "A critical node failure in the copper supply chain prevented the production of bronze weapons and ritual objects, causing immediate delegitimization of kings who could no longer reward followers or arm defenses, leading to rapid political fragmentation.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Cyprus", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Iran", "Italy"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Discovery of functional bronze foundries operating at full capacity in destroyed cities during the terminal phase", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "An atypical supply-chain shock model that prioritizes economic legitimacy over environmental or direct military causes."}

{"claim": "The collapse was primarily caused by a rapid and disruptive military innovation—the shift from chariot-based aristocratic warfare to massed, mobile infantry—which democratized violence and fatally undermined the political and economic structures of the palatial states.", "mechanism": "The palatial system was built on a military elite of chariot-owning aristocrats who controlled surplus and trade. The adoption of new infantry tactics and weapons (like the Naue II sword and javelin) by 'barbarian' groups and internal rebels allowed them to overwhelm the chariot armies. This led to the sacking of palaces, disruption of trade routes, and the collapse of the centralized redistributive economy.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence shows that chariot warfare remained the dominant and decisive military form throughout the 12th century BC, with no significant increase in infantry equipment.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian records from the period attribute their successful defense against the Sea Peoples to their superior chariot corps, not to a rapid adoption of massed infantry tactics.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This account frames the collapse as a social and military revolution from below. Egypt's survival is explained by its rapid adaptation and successful deployment of similar infantry tactics and foreign mercenaries against the Sea Peoples, as recorded at the Battle of the Delta."}

{"claim": "Prolonged regional drought 1250-1100 BC (evidenced by stable isotope and pollen studies) caused systemic crop failure, breaking the redistributive palace economy's legitimacy when grain stores proved insufficient, while Egypt survived through Nile flood reliability and emergency centralization under 20th Dynasty temple estates.", "mechanism": "Palace economies depended on intensive cereal agriculture and olive cultivation vulnerable to multi-decadal aridification; drought eliminated surplus extraction, triggering elite fragmentation, weaponization of marginal populations, and abandonment of administrative centers; Egypt's Nile hydrology buffered rainfall variation, and Ramesses III restructured grain collection through Theban and Memphis temple networks.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean palace abandonment correlating with local speleothem drought signals", "Hittite grain shortages in Amarna and Hattusa letters c.1250 BC", "Ugarit emergency agricultural texts and shipwreck evidence of grain desperation", "Levantine settlement shrinkage toward coastal plain", "Egyptian Wilbour Papyrus temple estate consolidation", "Anatolian highland pastoralism expansion post-1180 BC"], "excludes": ["Cyprus copper economy stability", "Assyrian expansion in Mesopotamia", "Egyptian political fragmentation under Ramesses XI", "Philistine settlement as non-drought phenomenon"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Nile flood levels from Roda nilometer and paleo-floodplain studies show reduced inundation 1150-1000 BC comparable to drought impacts elsewhere, yet Egypt persisted without palace collapse, suggesting hydrological buffer insufficient alone", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Mycenaean Pylos Linear B tablets record massive mobilization for coastal defense, not agricultural emergency, immediately pre-destruction; no palatial drought response documentation comparable to Hittite grain letters", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Ugarit and Hattusa show no skeletal evidence of famine mortality or settlement abandonment patterns typical of drought collapse (contra Tell Leilan); destruction layers indicate violence, not starvation abandonment", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egypt's 20th Dynasty temple estate expansion occurred under Ramesses III precisely when external threats peaked, suggesting military-not-climate causation for centralization; grain redistribution systems pre-existed alleged drought adaptation", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Follows Weiss 2012, Kaniewski et al. 2013, Langgut et al. 2013 paleoclimate synthesis; criticized for chronological imprecision (drought peaks c.300 years later in some proxies), and for assuming climate determinism that underweights political response variation. Egypt survival attributed to institutional flexibility rather than mere geography."}

{"claim": "Climate-induced famine drove migratory pressure that overwhelmed static defenses.", "mechanism": "Prolonged drought forced pastoralist groups to migrate sequentially, toppling states unable to absorb refugees.", "scope": {"covers": ["Levantine city-states", "Ugarit", "Hittite Anatolia"], "excludes": ["Nile Delta region", "Assyria"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Paleoclimate data indicates normal rainfall levels in Anatolia during 1200-1175 BC.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Engages A9 by explicitly excluding Assyria despite the proposed west-to-east migratory vector."}

{"claim": "A highly contagious and lethal pandemic, originating from a zoonotic spillover event, swept through the densely populated and interconnected palatial centers of the Eastern Mediterranean, causing a catastrophic demographic collapse that unraveled the economic and administrative fabric of the states.", "mechanism": "Biological/epidemiological forcing: The palatial system's dense urban centers, long-distance trade routes, and diplomatic missions (including royal marriages and exchanges of physicians) created an ideal network for a 'virgin soil' epidemic. Mass mortality led to a loss of specialized labor, administrative knowledge, and military manpower, causing the system to implode from within.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Bioarchaeological analysis of human remains from collapse-era destruction layers across the affected regions reveals no evidence of a shared, lethal pathogen, and ancient DNA analysis is negative for a pandemic-causing agent.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This is a more speculative, atypical candidate that draws parallels to the Plague of Justinian or the Antonine Plague. It accounts for the suddenness and the 'systems collapse' nature of the event, where the administrative structure vanishes without a single external conqueror. Egypt's survival is attributed to its larger population base, which could absorb demographic shock, and its more dispersed settlement pattern along the Nile, which limited transmission compared to the compact palatial centers."}

{"claim": "The collapse was triggered by a rapid shift to massed infantry tactics (the 'Infantry Revolution'), which rendered the chariot-based military aristocracies of the palatial states obsolete.", "mechanism": "Barbarian groups (Sea Peoples) adopted flexible, massed infantry with javelins and slashing swords, overwhelming the expensive, brittle chariot armies. Palatial states could not adapt institutionally because their political power was tied to chariot elites, leading to military defeat and administrative collapse.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Egyptian records depict Ramesses III's victory over the Sea Peoples as a conventional chariot battle, not an infantry swarm.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Explains the synchronous military overmatch across different palatial cultures while accounting for Egyptian survival through their rapid adoption of foreign mercenaries and infantry."}

{"claim": "Systems of coerced maritime and overland labor migration (the 'Sea Peoples' as organized displaced populations rather than ethnonyms) cascaded through alliance networks when palaces failed to absorb or redirect flows, destroying centers sequentially while Egyptian military-fiscal extraction from Nubia and internal delta fortification permitted territorial contraction without sovereignty loss.", "mechanism": "Migration pressure as strategic weaponization by displaced actors → network collapse via alliance commitment traps (defensive obligations activated simultaneously) → selective state survival through fungible resource substitution and defensive reorganization.", "scope": {"covers": ["Ugarit as network node", "Hittite western periphery", "Mycenaean coastal polities", "Sea Peoples as migration phenomenon"], "excludes": ["Egyptian core territory", "Anatolian plateau interior", "inland Levant beyond corridor zones"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Contemporary documentation identifies Sea Peoples as state-directed armies with stable command structures rather than migratory coalitions", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Ugarit or Hittite records show collapse preceding rather than coinciding with migration intensification", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian survival correlates with absence of Sea Peoples assault rather than successful military containment", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Network analysis demonstrates sparse rather than dense inter-palace alliance commitments in period 1250-1200 BC", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Diplomatic brief style: human-agency/network approach (Cline, Knapp & Manning lineage). Criticizable for assuming migration agency and for Egyptian exceptionalism via Nubian gold that A6 directly flags as scope-violating in nearby refutation."}

{"claim": "The collapse was an intentional, coordinated insurgency by subject populations and marginalized groups exploiting the weakening of central military monopolies.", "mechanism": "Asymmetric warfare and class revolt: The diffusion of iron-working allowed non-elites to arm themselves, breaking the chariot-aristocracy's monopoly on violence.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Sea Peoples as external invaders"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Absence of iron weapons in destruction layers predating the final collapse of the palaces.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "An atypical social-revolutionary take that reframes 'Sea Peoples' not as invaders but as displaced internal refugees or rebels."}

{"claim": "The collapse resulted from a punctuated cascade of maritime invasion initiated by competitive raiding between proto-Etruscan/Italic and western Anatolian maritime entrepreneurs, amplified by palace mercenary employment that created trained, mobile military forces who turned on paymasters", "mechanism": "Punctuated puncture: Sea Peoples as symptom not cause.
